text: The surgeon inserts a cystoscope into the urethra, visually identifying the opening of the vagina at the level of the pulpal urethra.
The epithelium of the vagina is visible. A ureteral catheter is inserted into the vagina and positioned to facilitate identification during the abdominal approach.
The robotic phase of the procedure begins with a longitudinal incision in the parietal peritoneum, exposing the bladder. The bladder is then suspended on the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon uses the light from the cystoscope as a visual guide to locate anatomical structures within the pelvic space during the robotic vaginectomy procedure.

A traction suture is placed on the apex of the vagina. The surgeon begins releasing the vagina from adhesions with surrounding structures, starting on the right side and then moving to the left. The dissection continues until reaching the attachment of the urethra. The vagina is completely freed, revealing the urethral catheter inside.
The surgeon removes the right part of the vaginal tissue, placing a stitch at the level of the vaginal stump.

The surgeon removes the left part of the vagina and places an additional stitch to reinforce the suture line. Hemostatic foam is applied to the operating site to control bleeding at the end of the procedure.
